item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just can't.

+ show block
– hide block

I hope this gets updated with an addendum that Stacey Lee (3009-C) gets released. :'(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ah i love this so much, stacey lee is such an interesting scp concept/character and i can’t wait to see more of her

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fun idea for the online personas that people create! i am thou.. thou art i..

There's a mistake here I believe

it is indeed a rather a fascinating inquiry

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is a really good article in that it approaches the anomaly from a bunch of different perspectives and takes the time to develop the character. for a first article this is stellar

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can clearly see that this is your first article, to be honest. There is a lot of lines that seem off, or in some way awkward for SCP standards. The clinical tone is always a challenge, and though it was far from the worst in getting into it, it was still clearly a newbie work.

This isn't bad, in the slightest. Had you been too focused on the tone, you would probably miss the actual selling points of your article, and they are pretty good. A good story, a good mystery, a good dilemma, it gets the reader engaged to the end.

For now, I do suggest to add -A to the sentient account. There are other tweaks that can improve the article, but it is already an easy upvote, so don't sweat too much on this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm embarrassed to admit I'm only now getting to this one, but I'm happy to say that it entirely lives up to the hype. It's a virtually perfect modern fable, deeply resonant and completely contemporary. There's a flawless balance here between story as metaphor and story as story, which is ridiculously impressive for a first outing.

On a separate but related note, Netflix released a film called Cam several months after this was posted. The movie shares a lot of conceptual similarities with 3009, and much of the critical praise for the film's ideas could just as easily be applied to this article. Once again, the wiki is ahead of the curve.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is incredible. a genius allegory on how social media warps people's public image and self esteem. one of the best skips ive read. +100

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was really good! I just kinda thought it would include Snapple. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I truly like this one. I instinctively rolled my eyes at the Snapchat image but it works to sort of throw the reader off as they read through the page. I thought that the idea of a digital doppelganger and that the IRL person is the original, but I really liked the exploration of the girl's psyche as it deteriorates as a result of prolonged confinement and the idea that she isn't who she thinks she is.

The very last paragraph got to me. They know that they're hurting her by forcing her to stay (making her take medication), there's strong evidence to suggest she's not a threat, and they know that it's wrong on some level but they're keeping her indefinitely. Giving the Foundation a heart is what I think makes stories better. They are not trying to be cold and cruel, but they truly believe that they're doing what is best for the world.

The final note brought tears to my eyes.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could both be real? It seemed to be a metaphor for how fake an image a person can present of themselves on social media. While a person can cultivate their internet persona to be better than their real self with ease, when -C was presented with how careless, confident, and happy she puts herself out to be on the internet in the context of it potentially being the "real" her, it made her feel as though the "her" in the phone was the real one, when in reality, it was just because her online persona was so different from her real personality. I don't actually necessarily think this was the author's intent, but this thematic message is interesting to entertain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hello I have so many questions about the existence of an on-site burger King.

Is it affiliated with the real Burger King? Is it a copy? Did they just start up a franchise and cover up its location? Where do the burger ingredients come from? Do they make cute SCP toys for the kids meals? Are there exclusive burger recipes? Where do they get the workers from? Is the entirety of Burger King a Foundation front? Was the on-site Burger King affected by the 2014 merger with Tim Horton's? Do they have a contract with Coca-Cola Company or can they serve Pepsi too? Would they amnesticize line workers serving Pepsi? Or at all? Are there teens working half-time in the on-site Burger King, hoping to find a better job soon? Are they paid minimum wage?

Those three words make me wonder so much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice skip you got here. And to think that this is your first one, Wow…

I've read in a guide1 here that first-timers don't usually do good, but if it does do good, it's rare. And this is one of 'em.

It deserves my +1.

P.S.: Is that you on the pic?

Footnotes
1. How to write an SCP, to be precise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was one of the first new SCPs that I had read after a long haitus from casual lurking of the Series 1 stuff back in my middle school creepypasta days, and I was completely blown away. Never got around to leaving a comment because I read this before my account application was approved, so I figured I would sing this article's praises now that I'd stumbled across it again as an editor's pick. Damn good job, and I can't wait to see what else you've got in store for the SCP community.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought at first that this was going to be a whimsical contrast with all the dark 3000 contest entries it butts shoulders with in the list, but it ended up so depressing. Great job!

(BTW what is up with the second ratings module?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is absolutely fantastic. If I ever made a list of my favorite SCPs of all time, this would be a very strong contender for #1.

I love the contrast in tone. Some parts of this are cute and bright.. almost manic. The end is crushingly depressing. This is exactly the kind of thing I wish I could write.

I love how it makes you question whether the Foundation is truly doing the right thing. I love how *human* all the characters seem.

I cant wait to see what you write next!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Somebody in Page 2 of the discussion proposed MY preferred theory: SCP-3009-C is indeed the original. But 3009 isn't exactly a "fake" either. It is in fact HER ONLINE PERSONA ITSELF which somehow developed a consciousness of its own and split off from her. Many people online portray themselves very differently from their real-life personalities. This girl could possess some kind of anomalous nature which causes "masks" she creates (i.e. fake personalities) to take on a mind and existence of their own.

This could also justify her continued containment, as who knows what other "masks" she may adopt (online or otherwise) if she existed out in the world continuing to socialize like a normal person. She could easily end up spawning further anomalies that split off from her main personality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This here is the headcanon I am going with for this article.

Also, if this were true, we can also justify the continued containment of 3009-C, as in this case both 3009 and 3009-C are anomalous. Clearly, a person whose online persona split off from their real self and became its own entity must be an anomaly in her own right. She could even potentially be dangerous; if she were to exist out in the world and develop other "masks" or secondary personalities she puts on as an act, those too could end up splitting off from her in various ways and becoming new anomalies; and there is no way to predict what those anomalies might be or what kind of danger they might pose. So yes, her continued confinement would actually be quite justified in this case, even if she IS just some poor girl with no control over her situation.

Some further evidence for your theory based on the info given:
Notice the difference in favorite colors cited by the two Staceys. 3009 said "light pink," the most stereotypically "girly" of all colors; this would make sense of 3009 is her attempt to exude a "bubbly and girly" persona online, as opposed to her in-real-life, more subdued personality which prefers "pastel purple."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is incredible for a first article, you did an amazing job! It ticked all the right boxes—a sense of the bizarre, mystery, suspense, horror, and pure feels.

Kudos for writing an article that made me think so much. A very enthusiastic +1 from this guy right here :) As others have said, when I read this article (and saw how quickly it has passed +100) I expected that it had been written by one of the "big authors," and was very (pleasantly) surprised to find here in the discussion that this was your very first article. To say that I'm "impressed" would be an understatement.

My personal theory is an elaboration of my own upon one proposed in page 2 of this discussion, that Stacey Lee (i.e. SCP 3009-C) is a person who developed a "mask," a false online persona more outgoing and bubbly than her real-life persona—of course, many people really do this. Due perhaps to some computational anomaly in her phone or the internet, or more likely due to some anomaly originating from Stacey herself, her mask-persona took on a mind of its own and split off from her entirely.

What I like best about this theory is that it fully justifies the continued containment of 3009-C. After all, if this theory is correct, then who knows what other anomalies she might spawn someday if she were allowed to roam free and socialize; she may develop other "masks" for interacting with the world, both online and otherwise, and that could pose a genuine threat depending upon what personalities those masks of hers have.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do like how this is a lil inversion 1192, since more sympathy is invoked by the presumbed original than the presumed copy, rather than the inverse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a very well written article, with a great theme, and it genuinely made me feel depressed. Good job.

My only complaint is the way that Foundation researchers repeatedly directly refer to the anomalous subjects by their SCP number designation instead of using their names. It feels really inconsistent with the otherwise friendly and relatively informal tone of the interview and experiment logs, and it feels counterintuitive for the Foundation to actively dehumanize people like that when it also seems to be genuinely concerned about maintaining their mental health.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A fun little idea of a skip that turns hella real by the end. Poor 3009-C. Anomalous or not, she deserves to be free.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The thing about the chatlog between Phone!Lee and Human!Lee is Phone!Lee isn't majorly concerned about presenting herself as real - she's taking the time to plant the idea in Human!Lee's head that NEITHER of them might be the original one. From a King Solomon and the Baby POV, I'd say Human!Lee is more entitled to their original life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, what I'm thinking is that Phone!Lee is the fake, while Real!Lee is… well, real. She's been trapped in a testing facility for lord knows how long, so it makes sense that her personality has changed. Meanwhile, Phone!Lee acts the same as the original — almost as if her personality is a snapshot (pun intended) of the original at a certain moment in time. Real!Lee changed, while Phone!Lee didn't, making the latter fit the Foundation's outdated info better.

Just my thoughts, obviously. It's a very good scip in any case :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In the fourth addendum, you refer to the conversation as Experiment 3009-6 when you probably meant -B-5. But that minor error aside, I've gotta give a +1 to this unexpected feels trip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahaha, thanks for the feedback, glad it affected you emotionally!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks so much for the feedback! I'm really glad that you liked the skip — I wasn't involved in SCP back in series 1 but when I first got into this, I basically read through series 1 in numerical order. I'm so happy that some of the best parts of series 1 bled through!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you liked it!! Thanks so much for the feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you so much! I've significantly edited the journal excerpt — I think it sounds much more natural now, haha. To be honest, I was a little concerned about the flow of that as well, so I'm glad that you mentioned it. The photo's definitely staying, because, well. I mean. I don't have anything to replace it with!

Anyway, thanks again for the excellent feedback, I really appreciate it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: what a sad ending omg

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wish we could upvote comments. That's how I interpret it too—A is the person she pretended to be online, and C is the person she actually is.

The fact that her friends and family predicted A's answers better than C's answers is something that almost depressed me the most.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tragic and absolutely outstanding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No problem!

For sure Stacey can write more seriously in her journal - I think it's just the writing style that is a bit off. This is very tricky to nail (and explain) but let me try. Some of it reads a little like an internal monologue from a heavy novel, too planned out, too perfect for a journal. There are a few lines which in particular stood out and hopefully reflect what I'm getting at:

  • "My identity is slipping away with every day I spend here."
  • "I drag myself through the day like I’m walking through treacle, waiting for time to trickle by."
  • "I bet they'll think this is evidence that I'm the fake one, since she types in omg text speak!!1! all the time and look, here's paragraphs and paragraphs. And maybe they'd be right." - this bit is written very dramatically (mainly the last sentence)
  • She's a better version of me, preserved forever in digital form like a butterfly crystallised in amber.

This is personal opinion of course, but I think if you toned down those kind of lines in the journal and made the rest of the text in there sound a little more natural (using more contractions, etc) - I think it might help. Not text speak or silliness but just a better flow. Maybe it would be worth doing an experiment draft just to see? Try putting yourself in her shoes and just write down the thoughts that come into her head as she would probably write them as they popped into her head. I guess it feels a bit too pre-planned right now.

It's not a big issue as overall the piece works very well, but that's what feedback's for, right?

I absolutely agree that the photo really makes the piece. Yes it will be controversial but change is good on a site like this. It's fresh, it's funny, it makes it seem more real, it attracts your attention immediately and makes you want to learn more (even if people don't like it, they will still be intrigued). I would seriously not bow down to pressure to remove it, embrace it! And your article is doing very well anyway, keep that integrity to what your original vision was.

PS A word of warning, Soma is kinda scary/creepy. The monsters in it are more of an afterthought but the highlights are the exploration and the story. It's very System Shock, lots and lots of story told through logs, audio recordings etc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oops, fixed that. Glad you liked it — I think the article picture gave a lot of people emotional whiplash, haha! Would love to hear theories, actually, though I can't confirm any of them myself!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's me. What's your discord name? I didn't see you.

I mostly wanted to join the discord to promote my article lol no offense people there are cool too

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much for your detailed comment! I'm incredibly glad that you enjoyed my first ever skip so much, and that it made you feel real emotion — that's the dream with fiction, right? Thanks so much for taking the time to read and buy into the skip, and for taking the time to leave such a lovely comment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ahaha, thank you very much for the compliment! I haven't done much "serious" fiction writing, it's mostly just been academic essays for me. I took a creative writing class back when I was in high school, and I may or may not have written fanfiction at one point (cough cough) but that's about the extent of my fictional pursuits.

(Also, I think I saw you in the SCPD discord under the same name! That's you, right?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if they're both the real one. It's just a difference of wet vs. hard ware…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really great. It's a well told story, the premise is instantly relatable and provokes a lot of thought. It's a really well done piece of fiction by any standard, but what I love about this as an SCP is how seamlessly it blends my favorite aspects of series 1 - cruel dilemmas, a Foundation that's as scary or more than the things it's containing, existential horror - with more recent trends like a focus on relatable characters, a narrative over which those characters change, and a wide emotional spectrum (complete with moments of lightheadedness and fun). It's a fantastic contribution to the site, and makes me excited for what's to come as we move into series 5.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a great read, didn't expect an article that starts with a silly Snapchat picture to go ti feels later on. I have theories for either or both being the "real" one, but I think I'll keep them to myself for now.

I did notice something in the first Update (the italicized paragraph):
logged out of all instances of SCP-3009-5

Shouldn't that be SCP-3009-B?

Anyway, take your upvote +1

item.title: Re: This is an ice cream with all the flavors I like the most.
item.content:encoded:
Oh, got it. That's such great feedback! And also quite an easy fix. I might go back and edit some of that stuff out.

(also, this SCP has changed number due to a coordinated exchange between me and the previous occupant of 3009, so sorry for confusing anyone!)

item.title: Re: This is an ice cream with all the flavors I like the most.
item.content:encoded: By the way, the only downfall I found is the (quite common) mistake of your regular people using Foundation jargon. Usually people say Supernatural, freak, strange, weird, etc. Anomalous and certain other words briefly break the inmersion imo. Once again, that's something that happens to everybody here. I'll save this spot and point out these in the near future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I got lengthy in my response to this skip. Lengthy and... Emotional. I'll shove it in a collapsible for the sake of space.
– hide block

I saw this and was expecting a bit of a laugh, ya'know… Silly snapchat related tomfoolery!

I'm just kinda sitting at my computer right now, with this genuine heartwrenching empathy for a fictional character. Just- damnit man. Some may see the shift in Stacey's (C's) tone as she falls into a depression to be sudden but, she's sitting there cut off from her friends, family, and life. She's stuck wondering if she's actually real because there's what's essentially a "better" version of her throwing petty snides and remarks at her at how "out-of-character" she is and how she's a supposed actress.

That sorrow and complete loss of identity is what hit this home for me. I tend to read skips kinda briefly, I find it hard to get into most of them tbh. Particularly when the subjects are human. I hit that part and genuinely froze up in my seat.

This isn't just a skip that makes you feel sorrow or fear- hell no. This is a skip that throws you into it's place and makes you feel the anxiety and fear that Stacey (C) is suffering through.

I got to the part where it specified that Stacey had been put on suicide watch. I felt actual horror.

TL;DR- This was amazing, and I think it may be my new favourite. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you so much for your detailed feedback — will think about that journal entry especially! I don't think Stacey's "text speak" precludes her writing seriously in her own journal, but I do agree that the transition was a bit abrupt. Gotta work on that one, haha.

I'm so glad that you liked the article overall and found the dialogue convincing. I'm glad you liked the photo, too — seems to be the most controversial part of the article but I personally think it's funny so am leaving it in! What's a Snapchat article without someone's selfie, right?

I'll definitely check out the Soma, thanks for the rec!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(Actually the draft is done I'm just waiting when my OCD subsides to post it and I'm shamelessly promoting myself haha)

May I ask if you've got any other writing experience before? Beside the help of the very experienced people you credited above, I don't think this good execution could have been pulled through by someone totally new to writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oops, will fix that.

Thank you so much for your comment — I won't comment on the "realness" of the two since I think it's best for the reader (like you) to come to their own conclusion, but I do love your theory! Hope you enjoyed this!

item.title: Re: This is an ice cream with all the flavors I like the most.
item.content:encoded:
Thank you so much for your detailed feedback!! You've picked up on pretty much everything that I wanted to portray, although I won't comment on -A and -C's "realness" since I think it's best if the reader (like you) comes to their own conclusion! Your use of the phrase "a photograph of the soul" in your description of -A really rings true to me, though.

I'm so glad that you enjoyed this! Thank you for making me feel so welcomed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks so much! I leave interpretation of -A and -C up to the readers, haha, but you're right that that this skip is largely about the disconnect between our "IRL self" and our "online self"! I'm so glad that you enjoyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
plague i love you <3 <3 <3

hope to see R3 in action soon!! + remember to hit me up for that!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oops, my bad. Will fix that. Thanks so much — glad you enjoyed this!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much for this!! I'm glad that you found the characters compelling and enjoyed the story. Hope to see your article soon!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Haha, thank you so much! I'm flattered beyond words that you'd think this was written by one of the "big authors". Glad you enjoyed it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Soma is really good at conveying this theme of existentialism. I got chills while watching playthroughs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the instance of two or more SCP-3439-B instances existing, SCP-3439 will favour the instance which was created first.

You probably want to change this to In the event, as instance is already used twice in this sentence with a different meaning.

Overall, a strong first skip with good theme exploration and social media ethos. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So one of my theories is that 3439 is some alternate reality version of 3439-C that has become linked in some way to this reality, but the only way it can communicate is through Snapchat. That would explain how it can make photos depicting 3439-C even though -C isn’t actually there- it’s taking images of the alternate reality. But hey, that’s just a theory.

Also, in Interview 3439-B-3, the description starts with “SCP-3439 and SCP-3439” as opposed to “SCP-3439 and SCP-3439-C”.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I gotta admit the idea sounded a bit lame on its own in the concept forum. But the finished article really works. I presume you know people around that age or talk/talked like Stacey Lee because to me the dialogue sounds really convincing. You're left sympathising with both Stacey's, and its a pretty shitty situation for them to be in. Its a neat twist on the classic clone concept and I think the photo is great too, definitely adds humanity to the tale (on top of being funny).

Overall it's well written with the experiment logs/staff notes sounding realistic as well as the Stacey's. The only gripe I had with it is the journal entry which seemed a bit off-tone in writing style to what we came to learn about Stacey, and a bit exposition-dumpy. Other than that though I thought it was good and it kept me reading to the end! Kudos especially for your first SCP and doing something a little different (and props to your friend who was so keen to have her photo as part of the SCP!).

PS It made me think of a great videogame called Soma which covers related themes, highly recommend playing that or watching a LetsPlay on Youtube, it has a great story.

item.title: This is an ice cream with all the flavors I like the most.
item.content:encoded:
First of all, this, oh god yes, THIS is how you first article. A clever idea, drafting and a lot of feedback. The idea is goofy, which makes it much better; I'm a sucker for goofy articles that make the overall SCPverse far scarier.

Now, there were two things that I noticed that I consider were paramount to make the article far more appealing to me. The first one is that your characters made me think and, more importantly, made me care. For me, -C is the real Stacey because we, as human beings, as subject to change. We wear masks in society and these masks allow others to know us partially but they miss important details. -A is a frozen picture of -C's personality. She won't change because it is the closest thing I've imagined of a photograph of the soul. -C, on the other hand, is subject to change as every human being is. You can't expect a normal person to remain the same after these circumnstances.

And then second part, when I realized this and empathy kicked in and I wanted them to at least let her leave and keep her on watch, it was too late. The Foundation is not there to let potential anomalies (even if harmless) out there. It's a great depiction of the pack-rat-ish, hyper paranoid Foundation that many, even experienced writers, have failed to convey for me.

Anyways, here's a TL;DR for you author: I hope you keep writing here. Welcome!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My theory is that they're both her- A is her social media persona- smart, funny, smug. C is her "real" self, a depressed teen. Somehow she split into two, and her social media persona lived in that realm only.

This is a great skip by the way. Can't believe it's your first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is fucking amazing, I was wondering if there was gonna be a reveal of which one is anomalous at the end, but instead I got depression

Aside from a few minor nitpicks the writing is great and considering most of the scp fan base is teenagers this is probably gonna be super relatable for all of them (including myself)

I’m gonna give this a big, big, bigass +1 and I hope to see more from you in the future

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was going to post my first article, thought it was finally okay, but after reading this… Fuck this site has high standard.

Great job, though. I really like how psychologically realistic and grim Stacey Lee is. Like, fuck, I got chills.

item.title: Re: Staff post - Open
item.content:encoded: oops, sorry! someone tagged it with SCP and I removed it but then thought that I was meant to keep it so put it back haha…
